text: The patient is positioned in a right side up lateral position for the procedure. Multiple ports are visible on the patient's abdomen, facilitating access for robotic instruments. The surgical goal is to remove the entire adrenal gland along with its surrounding fat.

The camera shows the right adrenal mass located caudal to the liver. The liver is dissected and retracted cranially using a grasper inserted through a 5mm trocar in the epigastrium, exposing the diaphragm superior to the adrenal gland.
The surgeon exposes the inferior vena cava and the right adrenal vein, revealing these structures in the surgical field.

A surgical clip is applied to the adrenal vein, which is then cauterized laterally using bipolar energy before being divided. The surgeon continues to dissect the adrenal gland superiorly, revealing the diaphragm.

The surgeon cauterizes and incises the arteries supplying the adrenal gland medially. The adrenal gland is further released medially by dissecting it away from the inferior vena cava using a combination of cautery and sharp dissection.
The surgeon performs dissection just superior to the renal hilum, carefully navigating the final plane of dissection on the upper pole of the kidney while avoiding branches of the renal vessels.

The surgeon has removed the adrenal gland, including the mass and surrounding periadrenal fat. The surgical field is inspected for hemostasis, ensuring no active bleeding remains in the adrenalectomy bed.
The surgeon mobilizes the spleen by dividing its peritoneal reflections, followed by further mobilization of the spleen and pancreas to expose the diaphragm above the adrenal gland.
The surgeon carefully safeguards the splenic vessels in the pancreas while identifying and isolating the left adrenal vein. Bipolar energy is used to cauterize the adrenal vein, ensuring hemostasis.

A surgical clip is applied to the left adrenal vein, which is then divided. The surgeon performs medial dissection using bipolar diathermy, dividing small vessels located lateral to the cruce of the diaphragm.
The surgeon performs inferior dissection just cranial to the renal vein and artery, followed by lateral dissection along the surface of the upper pole of the kidney.

The surgeon removes the final surrounding attachments of the left adrenal gland. After the gland is completely excised, the adrenalectomy bed is inspected for hemostasis, ensuring no active bleeding is present.
